docs.title: CFD Engineer | Multiphysics Simulation | Basilisk & Python
About Me
docs.text:
I am an engineering professional focused on Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD), with hands-on experience in building and analyzing simulations involving fluid flow, heat transfer, and reactive systems.

My work focuses on understanding physical behavior through numerical modeling, especially in coupled multi-physics problems.

docs.title: Core Skills
docs.text:
• CFD Simulation (Basilisk-learning, OpenFOAM-learning)
  • Fluid Mechanics and Heat Transfer
  • Multiphysics Modeling (Reaction + Flow + Heat)
  • Numerical Methods (Finite Volume Method)
  • Python-learning (NumPy, Matplotlib for post-processing)
  • C Programming-learning (solver implementation)

docs.title: Project 1: Species Transport Solver
docs.text:
• Implemented scalar transport using diffusion and advection
  • Studied spreading and transport behavior of species
  • Built foundation for multi-physics simulations

docs.title: Project 2: Reactive Buoyancy Flow Simulation
docs.text:
• Developed a coupled CFD solver using Basilisk (C)
  • Modeled interaction between:
  • Species transport
  • Heat generation (reaction)
  • Buoyancy-driven fluid flow
  • Observed plume formation and thermal runaway behavior
  • Performed parametric study on thermal diffusivity (kappa)
  • Analyzed system using Rayleigh number scaling

docs.title: Key Highlights
docs.text:
• Simulated thermal runaway in reactive systems
  • Demonstrated buoyancy-driven plume formation
  • Built complete CFD workflow:
  • Solver → Simulation → Post-processing → Visualization
  • Generated plots and flow animations for analysis

docs.title: Engineering Applications
docs.text:
The work presented here is relevant to:

  • Combustion systems
  • Battery thermal runaway analysis
  • Fire and smoke plume modeling
  • Natural convection problems
  • Heat transfer systems

docs.title: What I Can Do
docs.text:
• Develop CFD solvers using Basilisk
  • Analyze multi-physics systems
  • Perform parametric and sensitivity studies
  • Interpret simulation results physically
  • Create clear visualizations and reports

docs.title: Currently Learning
docs.text:
• OpenFOAM (for industry-level CFD workflows)
  • doing personal projects using Basilisk (C)
  • Advanced turbulence modeling
  • Reduced Order Modeling (ROM)
  • Application of CFD in EV thermal management

docs.title: Contact
docs.text: Email: ajayn145@gmail.com GitHub: https://github.com/ajayn145 Portfolio website - https://ajayn145.github.io/cfd-portfolio/

docs.title: One-Line Summary
docs.text: I build and analyze CFD simulations to understand how fluid flow, heat transfer, and reactions interact in real-world engineering systems.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: During the development of the species transport solver, several numerical and implementation challenges were encountered. Addressing these issues was essential to obtaining a stable and physically meaningful solution.

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: The scalar field exhibited stripe-like oscillations instead of a smooth distribution.

docs.title: Root Cause
docs.text:
• Use of an inappropriate advection treatment
  • Numerical dispersion introduced by the scheme

docs.title: Resolution
docs.text:
• Replaced the initial approach with Basilisk’s advection() operator
  • Ensured proper flux-based transport

docs.title: Key Insight
docs.text: The choice of numerical scheme has a direct impact on solution stability and can introduce non-physical artifacts if not handled correctly.

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: The scalar field moved in an unintended direction (vertical instead of horizontal).

docs.title: Root Cause
docs.text:
• Incorrect assignment of face-centered velocity (uf)
  • Misinterpretation of staggered grid variables

docs.title: Resolution
docs.text:
Assigned velocity components explicitly:

foreach_face(x)
  uf.x[] = 0.2;

foreach_face(y)
  uf.y[] = 0.0;

docs.title: Key Insight
docs.text: Face-centered variables must be handled carefully, especially in staggered grid formulations.

docs.title: 3. Compilation Error Due to Variable Redefinition
Problem
docs.text: Compilation failed due to redefinition of the velocity field (uf).

docs.title: Root Cause
docs.text: • Manual declaration of uf while it was already defined in advection.h

docs.title: Resolution
docs.text:
• Removed redundant declaration
  • Relied on Basilisk’s internal definitions

docs.title: Key Insight
docs.text: Understanding the internal structure of the solver framework helps avoid conflicts and redundant definitions.

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Compilation error indicating an undefined reference to tracers.

docs.title: Root Cause
docs.text: • Basilisk requires a tracer list for scalar transport

docs.title: Resolution
docs.text:
Defined tracer explicitly:

scalar * tracers = {C};

docs.title: Key Insight
docs.text: Framework-specific requirements must be satisfied even if they are not immediately obvious.

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: The scalar field (blob) was not visible in the output visualization.

docs.title: Root Cause
docs.text:
• Improper visualization scaling
  • Very low magnitude values of concentration

docs.title: Resolution
docs.text:
• Adjusted visualization range (min and max)
  • Tuned initial condition for better visibility

docs.title: Key Insight
docs.text: Visualization parameters can significantly affect interpretation and may hide correct physical behavior.

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: The scalar field dissipated too quickly, losing its structure.

docs.title: Root Cause
docs.text:
• High diffusion coefficient
  • Strong reaction rate

docs.title: Resolution
docs.text:
• Reduced diffusion coefficient
  • Adjusted reaction rate constant

docs.title: Key Insight
docs.text: Physical parameters must be carefully balanced to capture meaningful system behavior.

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: The simulation became unstable or produced inaccurate results.

docs.title: Root Cause
docs.text: • Time step too large for explicit time integration

docs.title: Resolution
docs.text:
Reduced time step:

DT = 1e-4;

docs.title: Key Insight
docs.text: Time step size is critical for stability, especially in explicit schemes.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
These challenges highlight that CFD development involves more than solving equations. It requires:

  • Careful selection of numerical methods
  • Understanding of solver internals
  • Systematic debugging of both numerical and implementation issues

Resolving these problems significantly improved both the accuracy of the solution and the understanding of computational fluid dynamics principles.

docs.title: Model Extensions
docs.text:
• Extend to multi-species transport
  • Include interaction between species
  • Implement realistic reaction mechanisms

docs.title: Physics Enhancements
docs.text:
• Couple with Navier–Stokes equations
  • Add temperature equation (energy transport)
  • Simulate combustion-like problems

docs.title: Numerical Improvements
docs.text:
• Implement adaptive mesh refinement
  • Explore higher-order advection schemes
  • Improve time integration methods

docs.title: Application-Oriented Studies
docs.text:
• Pollutant dispersion simulations
  • HVAC airflow and contaminant transport
  • Heat transfer in fluid systems

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Future extensions of this work will move from simplified scalar transport toward fully coupled multiphysics simulations used in real-world engineering problems.

docs.title: What Equation is Being Solved?
docs.text:
This project solves a species transport equation, which describes how a substance (species) moves and changes inside a flowing medium.

The governing equation is:

∂C/∂t + ∇·(uC) = D∇²C − kC

Where:

  • C → concentration of the species
  • u → velocity field (flow direction and speed)
  • D → diffusion coefficient
  • k → reaction rate

docs.title: Physical Meaning of Each Term
docs.text:
• ∂C/∂t Change of concentration with time

  • ∇·(uC) Transport due to fluid motion (Advection)

  • D∇²C Spreading due to concentration gradients (Diffusion)

  • −kC Decay or consumption of species (Reaction)

docs.title: How This Relates to Real CFD Problems
docs.text:
In real-world simulations, this equation is used to model:

  • Pollutant transport in air and water
  • Mixing of different fluids
  • Heat transfer (temperature behaves like a scalar)
  • Combustion and chemical reactions

docs.title: Important Note
docs.text:
This project solves a single-species transport problem, which is a simplified version of real industrial simulations.

In advanced cases, multiple species interact with each other through complex reactions.

docs.title: Implementation
docs.text:
The solver is implemented using Basilisk C.

Key steps:

  • Initialize scalar field (Gaussian blob)
  • Define velocity field
  • Solve advection using Basilisk's advection module
  • Solve diffusion using diffusion operator
  • Apply reaction term

docs.title: 1. Numerical Schemes Matter
docs.text: Using a centered scheme for advection caused non-physical oscillations (stripes). This highlighted the importance of selecting stable numerical methods.

docs.title: 2. Difference Between Physical and Numerical Diffusion
docs.text:
• Physical diffusion: controlled by diffusivity (D)
  • Numerical diffusion: introduced by discretization

Understanding this distinction was critical to interpreting results.

docs.title: 3. Importance of Scaling in Visualization
docs.text: Incorrect scaling initially made the blob invisible. Proper min/max values are essential for meaningful visualization.

docs.title: 4. Mesh Resolution Effects
docs.text:
Increasing grid resolution (128 → 512):

  • Improved solution accuracy
  • Reduced numerical errors
  • Better captured gradients

docs.title: 5. Coupled Physics Understanding
docs.text:
This project demonstrated how:

  • Advection transports the scalar
  • Diffusion spreads it
  • Reaction reduces it

All three processes interact simultaneously.

docs.title: 6. Debugging CFD Simulations
docs.text:
Several issues were encountered and resolved:

  • Compilation errors due to missing includes
  • Instability due to large time steps
  • Incorrect field definitions (uf, Df)
  • Visualization issues

This significantly improved debugging and solver-level understanding.

docs.title: Model Simplifications
docs.text:
• Single-species transport model
  • No interaction between multiple species
  • Reaction modeled as simple first-order decay

docs.title: Flow Assumptions
docs.text:
• Constant velocity field
  • No coupling with Navier–Stokes equations
  • No turbulence effects

docs.title: Numerical Limitations
docs.text:
• Explicit time stepping requires small time steps
  • Grid resolution impacts accuracy
  • No adaptive mesh refinement used in this setup

docs.title: Physical Simplifications
docs.text:
• No temperature coupling (no energy equation)
  • Constant diffusion coefficient
  • No real chemical kinetics

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: This project focuses on understanding the fundamentals of scalar transport. While simplified, it provides a strong foundation for more complex CFD simulations.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
This section explains how the governing species transport equation is solved numerically.

The equation:

∂C/∂t + ∇·(uC) = D∇²C − kC

is solved using a finite-volume approach with time-stepping.

docs.title: Finite Volume Method (FVM)
docs.text:
The computational domain is divided into small control volumes (cells).

Why this method? - Ensures conservation of mass and species - Widely used in industrial CFD solvers

Concept: Each cell stores an average value of concentration (C), and fluxes across cell faces determine how C changes.

docs.title: Explicit Time Stepping
docs.text:
The solution is advanced in time using small time steps (Δt).

Why used here? - Simple to implement - Easy to understand for transient problems

Limitation: - Requires small time step for stability

docs.title: Equation Term:
docs.text: ∇·(uC)

docs.title: Numerical Treatment:
docs.text:
• Solved using Basilisk’s built-in advection() function
  • Uses a stable flux-based scheme

Why this is important: Initially, a naive or centered approach caused: - Non-physical oscillations - Stripe-like artifacts

Switching to a proper advection scheme: - Eliminated oscillations - Ensured stable transport

docs.title: Equation Term:
docs.text: D∇²C

docs.title: Numerical Treatment:
docs.text:
• Solved using Basilisk’s diffusion() function
  • Uses an implicit multigrid solver

Why implicit? - Stable even for larger time steps - Efficient for solving diffusion equations

docs.title: Equation Term:
docs.text: −kC

docs.title: Numerical Treatment:
docs.text: ```text C_new = C / (1 + k·dt)

docs.title: Objective
docs.text:
To develop a 2D finite-volume CFD solver for scalar transport including advection, diffusion, and reaction using Basilisk.

This project implements a simplified species transport solver, commonly used in CFD to simulate transport and reaction of scalar quantities.

docs.title: Problem Description
docs.text:
A scalar concentration field (C) is initialized as a Gaussian blob and transported in a uniform velocity field.

The governing equation solved is:

∂C/∂t + ∇·(uC) = D∇²C − kC

The scalar undergoes:

  • Advection (transport by flow)
  • Diffusion (spreading due to gradients)
  • Reaction (decay over time)

docs.title: In A NUTSHELL:--
docs.text:
Imagine you drop a drop of ink into flowing water.

Three things will happen:

  1. The ink will move with the water → this is Advection
  2. The ink will spread out and mix → this is Diffusion
  3. The ink will fade or disappear over time → this is Reaction

This project simulates exactly this process using mathematics and numerical methods.

docs.title: What is the Scalar here?
docs.text:
A scalar is simply a quantity that has a value at every point in space.

In this project:

  • C represents concentration (like ink density)
  • Each point in the domain has a value of C

docs.title: What is the Computational Domain here?
docs.text:
We simulate the problem inside a square region:

  • This is divided into many small cells (grid)
  • Each cell stores a value of concentration

docs.title: What is the Gaussian Blob here?
docs.text:
The initial condition is a "blob" of concentration.

Mathematically:

C = exp(-100r²)

This creates:

  • High concentration at center
  • Smooth decrease outward

👉 This gives a nice visible shape to track

docs.title: What Does the Solver Do?
docs.text:
At every time step:

  1. Moves the scalar → Advection
  2. Spreads it → Diffusion
  3. Reduces it → Reaction

This process repeats until the final time.

docs.title: Why is This Important?
docs.text:
This type of simulation is used in:

  • Pollution spreading in air/water
  • Heat transfer problems
  • Combustion modeling
  • HVAC airflow analysis

docs.title: Simple Summary
docs.text:
• We start with a blob
  • It moves, spreads, and fades
  • The computer calculates this step by step

docs.title: Advection (Transport by Flow)
docs.text: The scalar is transported by the velocity field without changing its shape significantly.

docs.title: Diffusion (Spreading)
docs.text: The scalar spreads from regions of high concentration to low concentration, smoothing the distribution.

docs.title: Reaction (Decay)
docs.text: The scalar concentration decreases over time due to a decay process.

docs.title: Key Insight
docs.text: The scalar field evolution depends strongly on both physical parameters and the numerical scheme used, which affects stability and accuracy.

docs.title: Result Analysis
docs.text: The simulation captures the expected physical behavior of a reacting scalar transport problem.

docs.title: Observed Behavior
docs.text:
• The scalar field moves in the direction of the velocity field (advection)
  • The concentration spreads over time (diffusion)
  • The magnitude of the scalar decreases gradually (reaction)

docs.title: Diffusion Coefficient (D)
docs.text:
• Higher values → faster spreading
  • Lower values → sharper concentration profile

docs.title: Reaction Rate (k)
docs.text:
• Higher values → faster decay of scalar
  • Lower values → longer persistence of the blob

docs.title: Grid Resolution
docs.text:
• Higher resolution (512) → better shape preservation
  • Lower resolution → increased numerical diffusion

docs.title: Time Step (Δt)
docs.text:
• Smaller time step → stable and accurate solution
  • Larger time step → potential instability

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
The results are consistent with theoretical expectations of the species transport equation:

  • Transport follows flow direction
  • Diffusion smooths gradients
  • Reaction reduces concentration

This confirms the correctness of the numerical implementation.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This project was developed using a combination of CFD frameworks, programming tools, and visualization utilities. Each tool was selected based on its role in solving the governing equations efficiently and transparently.

docs.title: Basilisk (CFD Library)
docs.text:
Why it was used: - Easy for learning and implementing the libraries. - Provides built-in numerical solvers for: - Advection - Diffusion - Multigrid methods - Allows direct implementation of governing equations - Offers fine control over numerical methods

Role in Project: - Solves the species transport equation - Handles grid generation and time-stepping - Provides efficient numerical operators

docs.title: C Language
docs.text:
Why it was used: - Required for working with Basilisk - Allows low-level control over numerical operations - High computational efficiency (important for CFD)

Role in Project: - Implements solver logic - Defines simulation events (initialization, advection, diffusion, reaction) - Controls simulation flow

docs.title: Finite Volume Method (FVM)
docs.text:
Why it was used: - Conserves physical quantities (mass, species) - Widely used in industrial CFD solvers

Role in Project: - Discretizes the governing equation over control volumes

docs.title: Explicit Time Stepping
docs.text:
Why it was used: - Simple to implement and understand - Suitable for transient simulations

Trade-off: - Requires small time step for stability

docs.title: Multigrid Solver (Diffusion)
docs.text: Why it was used: - Efficient for solving diffusion equations - Reduces computational cost compared to simple solvers

docs.title: Basilisk Output (output_ppm)
docs.text: Why it was used: - Built-in and easy to integrate with solver - Generates frame-by-frame visualization

docs.title: Video Conversion (ppm2mp4)
docs.text: Why it was used: - Converts simulation frames into a continuous video - Helps visualize transient behavior clearly

docs.title: Linux / WSL
docs.text: Why it was used: - Basilisk is best supported in Linux environments - Provides stable compilation and execution setup

docs.title: GCC / qcc Compiler
docs.text: Why it was used: - Required to compile Basilisk-based C programs - Optimized for numerical computations

docs.title: Git & GitHub
docs.text: Why it was used: - Tracks changes in code and documentation - Enables project versioning and sharing

docs.title: MkDocs + Material Theme
docs.text: Why it was used: - Converts project into structured documentation - Helps present work professionally - Easy deployment via GitHub Pages

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
Each tool in this project was selected based on its ability to support:

  • Accurate numerical simulation
  • Efficient computation
  • Clear visualization
  • Professional documentation

Together, they form a complete workflow similar to real-world CFD development pipelines.

docs.title: Problem:
docs.text:
• Temperature increased rapidly and became unbounded
  • Simulation produced unrealistic values

docs.title: Cause:
docs.text:
• Strong reaction term (exponential growth)
  • Insufficient diffusion

docs.title: Solution:
docs.text:
• Limited reaction rate (k_local)
  • Introduced minimum temperature threshold
  • Tuned heat release parameter (Q)

docs.title: Learning:
docs.text: Reaction-dominated systems can easily become unstable

docs.title: Problem:
docs.text: • Small changes in κ caused drastically different results

docs.title: Observation:
docs.text:
• Low κ → sharp gradients → instability
  • High κ → smooth solution

docs.title: Solution:
docs.text:
• Performed multiple simulations with different κ values
  • Compared results using plots

docs.title: Learning:
docs.text: Diffusion plays a critical role in stabilizing the system

docs.title: Problem:
docs.text:
• Unexpected flow behavior
  • Hard to understand cause-effect relationships

docs.title: Cause:
docs.text: Strong coupling between: - Temperature → buoyancy → velocity → transport → reaction

docs.title: Solution:
docs.text:
• Studied each term individually
  • Monitored key variables (Tmax, Csum, plume height)

docs.title: Learning:
docs.text: Multi-physics systems are highly nonlinear and interdependent

docs.title: Problem:
docs.text: • Only one log file was generated despite multiple runs

docs.title: Cause:
docs.text: • File naming was not parameterized properly

docs.title: Solution:
docs.text:
• Used dynamic filenames:log_kappa_%g.dat

  • Ensured each simulation writes separate output

docs.title: Learning:
docs.text: Proper data management is essential for parametric studies

docs.title: Problem:
docs.text:
• Missing plots
  • Incorrect file references

docs.title: Cause:
docs.text:
• File name mismatch (0.01 vs 0.001)
  • Duplicate code blocks

docs.title: Solution:
docs.text:
• Cleaned Python script
  • Verified file names
  • Structured plotting logic clearly

docs.title: Learning:
docs.text: Post-processing is as important as simulation

docs.title: Problem:
docs.text:
• Videos not appearing in website
  • 404 errors

docs.title: Cause:
docs.text:
• Incorrect file paths
  • Files not placed in correct directory

docs.title: Solution:
docs.text:
• Moved videos to proper folder:docs/project-2-.../images/

  • Updated markdown paths

docs.title: Learning:
docs.text: File organization is crucial for documentation

docs.title: Problem:
docs.text:
• Pages not loading
  • "404 Not Found" errors

docs.title: Cause:
docs.text:
• Incorrect paths in mkdocs.yml
  • Typographical mistakes

docs.title: Solution:
docs.text:
• Fixed navigation paths
  • Ensured folder structure matches config

docs.title: Learning:
docs.text: Documentation requires the same debugging mindset as coding

docs.title: Problem:
docs.text: • Difficulty interpreting results initially

docs.title: Solution:
docs.text:
• Related results to physical concepts:
  • Diffusion → smoothing
  • Reaction → heat generation
  • Buoyancy → plume rise

docs.title: Learning:
docs.text: CFD is not just coding — it is physical interpretation

docs.title: Problem:
docs.text: • Handling multiple plots and videos became messy

docs.title: Solution:
docs.text:
• Organized outputs:
  • Separate folders for images and videos
  • Clear naming conventions

docs.title: Learning:
docs.text: Structured workflow improves productivity

docs.title: Challenge:
docs.text: • Moving from simple transport to multi-physics system

docs.title: Differences:
docs.text:
• Project 1 → single equation
  • Project 2 → coupled system

docs.title: Learning:
docs.text: Understanding evolves significantly with complexity

docs.title: Final Takeaway
docs.text:
The biggest lesson from this project:

Debugging is not just fixing errors — it is understanding the physics, numerics, and implementation together.

Every challenge improved: - Problem-solving ability - Physical intuition - CFD understanding

docs.title: 1. Mesh Refinement Study (Grid Independence)
docs.text: In the current project, simulations were performed on a fixed grid resolution.

docs.title: Future Improvement:
docs.text:
• Run simulations with multiple grid sizes (e.g., 64, 128, 256)
  • Compare:
  • Maximum temperature
  • Plume structure
  • Reaction behavior

docs.title: Objective:
docs.text: To ensure that the solution is independent of mesh resolution

docs.title: Why it matters:
docs.text: A physically correct solution should not depend on the grid size

docs.title: 2. Time Step Sensitivity Analysis
docs.text: The simulation uses a fixed time step (DT).

docs.title: Future Improvement:
docs.text:
• Test different time steps:
  • Smaller DT → more accurate
  • Larger DT → faster but unstable

docs.title: Objective:
docs.text: • Identify stable and optimal time step range

docs.title: Key Insight:
docs.text: Stability and accuracy are strongly dependent on time discretization

docs.title: 3. More Realistic Reaction Model
docs.text: Current model uses a simplified Arrhenius-type reaction.

docs.title: Future Improvement:
docs.text:
• Include:
  • Multi-step chemical reactions
  • Species interaction
  • Heat loss mechanisms

docs.title: Objective:
docs.text: To better represent real chemical systems

docs.title: 4. Variable Fluid Properties
docs.text: Currently: - Viscosity (μ) and diffusivity (κ) are constant

docs.title: Future Improvement:
docs.text:
Make them temperature-dependent:

  • μ = μ(T)
  • κ = κ(T)

docs.title: Why important:
docs.text: In real systems: - Hot fluids behave differently than cold fluids

docs.title: 5. Inclusion of Heat Loss Mechanisms
docs.text: Currently: - Heat only increases due to reaction

docs.title: Future Improvement:
docs.text:
• Add heat loss:
  • Conduction to walls
  • Radiation effects
  • Convective cooling

docs.title: Objective:
docs.text: Prevent unrealistic temperature growth and improve physical realism

docs.title: 6. 3D Simulation Extension
docs.text: Current simulation is 2D.

docs.title: Future Improvement:
docs.text: • Extend to 3D domain

docs.title: Expected outcomes:
docs.text:
• More realistic plume structures
  • Complex vortex dynamics
  • Better representation of real flows

docs.title: Challenge:
docs.text: Increased computational cost

docs.title: 7. Turbulence Modeling
docs.text: Current flow is laminar.

docs.title: Future Improvement:
docs.text:
• Introduce turbulence models:
  • LES (Large Eddy Simulation)
  • DNS (Direct Numerical Simulation, if feasible)

docs.title: Objective:
docs.text: Capture: - Mixing enhancement - Turbulent plume behavior

docs.title: 8. Boundary Condition Improvements
docs.text: Currently: - Simple no-slip boundaries

docs.title: Future Improvement:
docs.text:
• Open boundary / outflow conditions
  • Inflow-driven systems
  • Realistic environmental conditions

docs.title: Future Improvement:
docs.text:
• Validate simulation results with:
  • Experimental data
  • Literature benchmarks

docs.title: Objective:
docs.text: Ensure physical correctness and credibility

docs.title: 10. Automation and Parametric Study
docs.text: Currently: - Simulations are run manually for different κ values

docs.title: Future Improvement:
docs.text:
• Automate runs using Python scripts
  • Perform parameter sweeps:
  • κ (diffusion)

docs.title: Outcome:

  • An industry-relevant tool

docs.title: What Equations Are Being Solved?
docs.text:
This project solves a coupled reactive flow problem involving:

  • Species transport
  • Heat transfer
  • Fluid flow
  • Buoyancy

docs.title: 1. Species Transport Equation
docs.text:
The governing equation is:

dC/dt + div(u*C) = D * laplacian(C) - R

Where:

  • C → species concentration
  • u → velocity field
  • D → diffusion coefficient
  • R → reaction rate

docs.title: Physical Meaning of Each Term
docs.text:
• dC/dt → change of concentration with time
  • div(u*C) → transport due to fluid motion (advection)
  • D * laplacian(C) → spreading due to diffusion
  • -R → consumption of species due to reaction

docs.title: 2. Temperature Equation
docs.text:
The temperature evolution is given by:

dT/dt + div(u*T) = kappa * laplacian(T) + Q * R

Where:

  • T → temperature
  • kappa → thermal diffusivity
  • Q → heat release coefficient

docs.title: Interpretation
docs.text:
• Temperature spreads due to diffusion
  • Reaction generates heat
  • Fluid flow transports heat

docs.title: 3. Reaction Model
docs.text:
The reaction rate is defined as:

R = k0 * C * exp(-Ea / T)

Where:

  • k0 → reaction constant
  • Ea → activation energy
  • T → temperature

docs.title: Key Insight
docs.text:
• Low T → slow reaction
  • High T → very fast reaction

This leads to thermal runaway behavior.

docs.title: 4. Momentum Equation (Navier–Stokes)
docs.text:
The velocity field follows:

du/dt + (u · grad)u = -grad(p) + nu * laplacian(u) + buoyancy

And:

div(u) = 0

Where:

  • u → velocity
  • p → pressure
  • nu → kinematic viscosity

docs.title: 5. Buoyancy Model
docs.text:
Buoyancy force is modeled as:

buoyancy = T (in vertical direction)

docs.title: Interpretation
docs.text:
• Hot fluid rises
  • Cold fluid sinks

This creates natural convection (plume formation).

docs.title: 6. Coupling Between Equations
docs.text:
The system is strongly coupled:

  • Reaction consumes species → increases temperature
  • Temperature increases → accelerates reaction
  • Temperature generates buoyancy → induces flow
  • Flow transports species and temperature

docs.title: Feedback Loop
docs.text: Temperature ↑ → Reaction ↑ → Temperature ↑

docs.title: 7. Rayleigh Number (Physical Insight)
docs.text:
Rayleigh number is estimated as:

Ra = T / (nu * kappa)

docs.title: Meaning
docs.text:
• High Ra → strong convection
  • Low Ra → stable diffusion-dominated flow

docs.title: 8. Boundary Conditions
docs.text:
• Velocity: no-slip (u = 0 at all walls)
  • Scalars (C, T): initialized as Gaussian distribution
  • No inflow or outflow

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
This is a nonlinear coupled system where:

  • Diffusion stabilizes
  • Reaction destabilizes
  • Buoyancy redistributes energy

This interaction produces complex plume structures similar to real engineering systems.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
This section explains how the governing equations are implemented in code using the Basilisk framework.

The implementation is event-based, where each physical process is handled in a structured sequence.

docs.title: 1. Overall Structure of the Code
docs.text:
The solver is written using Basilisk’s event-driven architecture.

Main components:

  • Field definitions
  • Initialization
  • Physical processes (advection, diffusion, reaction)
  • Fluid solver
  • Output

Each part is handled using "events".

docs.title: 2. Field Definitions
docs.text:
The following variables are defined:

  • C → species concentration
  • T → temperature
  • u → velocity field
  • p → pressure

In code:

scalar C[], T[];

These represent values stored at each grid cell.

docs.title: 3. Initialization
docs.text:
The simulation starts by defining initial conditions.

A Gaussian distribution is used:

  • Species is concentrated in a small region
  • Temperature follows the same distribution

Meaning:

  • A "blob" is created at the start
  • This blob evolves over time

docs.title: 4. Time Loop (Core Execution)
docs.text:
The simulation progresses step-by-step in time.

At each time step, the following processes occur:

  1. Advection
  2. Diffusion
  3. Reaction
  4. Fluid flow update
  5. Buoyancy effect
  6. Output

docs.title: 5. Advection Implementation
docs.text:
Advection moves quantities with the fluid.

In code:

advection({C, T}, u, dt);

Meaning:

  • Species and temperature are transported
  • Movement depends on velocity field

docs.title: 6. Diffusion Implementation
docs.text:
Diffusion spreads quantities from high to low values.

In code:

diffusion(C, dt, Df); diffusion(T, dt, Df);

Meaning:

  • C spreads using diffusion coefficient D
  • T spreads using thermal diffusivity kappa

docs.title: 7. Reaction Implementation
docs.text:
Reaction is computed explicitly at each cell.

Steps:

  1. Compute reaction rate:

k = k0 * exp(-Ea / T)

  1. Compute change in species:

dC = k * C * dt

  1. Update values:

C = C - dC T = T + Q * dC

Meaning:

  • Species is consumed
  • Temperature increases

docs.title: 8. Fluid Solver (Navier–Stokes)
docs.text:
Fluid motion is handled using Basilisk’s built-in solver.

Included using:

docs.title: include "navier-stokes/centered.h"
docs.text:
Meaning:

  • Velocity and pressure are solved internally
  • No need to manually implement equations

docs.title: 9. Buoyancy Implementation
docs.text:
Buoyancy is added as a force in the vertical direction.

In code:

av.y[] = (T[] + T[-1]) / 2;

Meaning:

  • Temperature generates upward force
  • Hot fluid rises

docs.title: 10. Boundary Conditions
docs.text:
Velocity:

  • No-slip condition
  • Fluid velocity is zero at walls

Scalars (C and T):

  • Initialized at the beginning
  • No external inflow or outflow

docs.title: 11. Output Generation
docs.text:
Results are saved at regular time intervals.

Outputs include:

  • Species field (C)
  • Temperature field (T)
  • Velocity magnitude
  • Vorticity

Also, videos are generated using:

ppm2mp4

docs.title: 12. Data Logging
docs.text:
Key quantities are tracked over time:

  • Maximum temperature
  • Total species mass
  • Plume height

These are stored in log files for analysis.

docs.title: 13. Workflow Summary
docs.text:
At each time step:

  1. Transport (advection)
  2. Spread (diffusion)
  3. React (reaction)
  4. Move fluid (Navier–Stokes)
  5. Apply buoyancy
  6. Save results

docs.title: 14. Key Implementation Insight
docs.text:
This solver does not just solve separate equations.

It combines:

  • Reaction
  • Heat transfer
  • Fluid motion

All interacting together.

docs.title: Final Understanding
docs.text:
The implementation shows how a complex physical system can be built step-by-step using:

  • Simple operations
  • Clear sequence of processes
  • Strong coupling between physics

docs.title: One-Line Summary
docs.text: The code simulates how species reacts, heat is generated, and fluid moves, by updating each physical process step-by-step in time.

docs.title: Learnings
docs.text: This introduced strong temperature dependence.

docs.title: Learning:
docs.text:
• Reaction is not linear
  • Small increase in temperature → large increase in reaction rate

To maintain stability: - Reaction rate was capped - Temperature lower bound was enforced

This highlighted:

Numerical stability must sometimes be enforced manually

docs.title: 5. Buoyancy Drives Flow Formation
docs.text:
Buoyancy was modeled as: a_y ∝ T

This resulted in: - Rising plume formation - Vortex structures - Flow instabilities

docs.title: Key understanding:
docs.text:
Temperature differences create fluid motion even without external forcing

This is fundamental in: - Natural convection - Atmospheric flows - Fire dynamics

docs.title: 6. Emergence of Complex Flow Structures
docs.text:
Even with simple initial conditions (Gaussian blob), the system developed:

  • Mushroom-shaped plumes
  • Vortices
  • Mixing layers

This shows:

Complex flow behavior can emerge from simple equations

This is one of the most fascinating aspects of CFD.

docs.title: 7. Numerical Sensitivity and Stability
docs.text:
The simulation required careful tuning of:

  • Time step (DT)
  • Diffusion coefficients
  • Reaction parameters

If not handled properly: - Solution diverges - Temperature blows up - Simulation becomes unstable

docs.title: Key takeaway:
docs.text: Stability is as important as accuracy in CFD

docs.title: 8. Importance of Monitoring Quantities
docs.text:
Tracking the following quantities was crucial:

  • Maximum temperature (Tmax)
  • Total species (Csum)
  • Plume height (center of mass)

These helped in: - Understanding system behavior - Comparing different cases - Identifying instability regions

docs.title: 9. Rayleigh Number as a Physical Indicator
docs.text:
Rayleigh number was computed as: Ra ∝ Tmax / (nu * kappa)

This provided insight into:

  • Strength of buoyancy-driven flow
  • Transition from stable to unstable regimes

docs.title: Learning:
docs.text: Non-dimensional numbers help interpret physical behavior

docs.title: Project 1:
docs.text:
• Only species transport
  • No flow coupling
  • Simpler physics

docs.title: Project 2:
docs.text:
• Fully coupled system
  • Includes velocity, temperature, reaction, buoyancy
  • Highly nonlinear behavior

docs.title: Key evolution:
docs.text: From solving a single equation → to solving a multi-physics coupled system

docs.title: 11. Understanding Through Visualization
docs.text:
Visual outputs (videos and plots) played a major role:

  • Helped observe plume evolution
  • Showed vortex formation
  • Revealed instability patterns

docs.title: Learning:
docs.text: Visualization is essential to understand CFD results

docs.title: 12. Practical CFD Insight Gained
docs.text:
By completing this project, I gained:

  • Understanding of multi-physics coupling
  • Experience with instability mechanisms
  • Insight into convection-diffusion-reaction systems
  • Ability to interpret simulation results physically

docs.title: Final Takeaway
docs.text:
This project demonstrated that:

CFD is not just solving equations — it is understanding how physics, numerics, and computation interact.

Small parameter changes can lead to: - Stable diffusion-dominated flow OR - Violent convection-driven instability

Understanding this balance is key to real-world simulations.

docs.title: Why Do We Need a Numerical Method?
docs.text:
The equations we are solving (fluid flow + heat + reaction) are:

  • Very complex
  • Nonlinear
  • Coupled together

This means we cannot solve them exactly using formulas.

So instead, we:

  • Break the domain into small pieces
  • Solve equations approximately
  • March forward in time

This is called a numerical method.

docs.title: 1. Governing Idea
docs.text:
We solve equations of the form: 

Change = Transport + Diffusion + Reaction

For species (C): 

∂C/∂t + ∇·(uC) = D∇²C - kC

For temperature (T): 

∂T/∂t + ∇·(uT) = κ∇²T + QkC

docs.title: ∂C/∂t
docs.text:
• Change of concentration with time
  • "How fast is it changing?"

docs.title: ∇·(uC)
docs.text: Advection (movement by fluid) * u → velocity (how fast fluid moves) * C → how much species is present Fluid carries the species along with it

docs.title: D∇²C
docs.text: Diffusion * D → diffusion coefficient * ∇² → spreading operator Species spreads from high concentration → low concentration

docs.title: -kC
docs.text: Reaction (consumption) * k → reaction rate * C → amount of species Species gets consumed

docs.title: QkC (Temperature equation)
docs.text: Heat generation due to reaction * Q → heat released per reaction * kC → reaction strength More reaction = more heat

docs.title: 2. Domain Discretization (Breaking the Space)
docs.text:
We divide the domain into small squares:

|---|---|---|
|   |   |   |
|---|---|---|
|   |   |   |
|---|---|---|

Each box = control volume (cell)

docs.title: What is stored in each cell?
docs.text: Each cell contains: * Velocity (u) * Pressure (p) * Species (C) * Temperature (T)

docs.title: 3. Finite Volume Method (Core Idea)
docs.text: Instead of solving equations at a point: We solve them over a small box

docs.title: Fundamental Equation
docs.text:
For any cell: 

Rate of change inside cell = Flux entering - Flux leaving + Source

docs.title: Break it down:
docs.text:
• Flux entering → stuff coming in
  • Flux leaving → stuff going out
  • Source → generation (reaction, heat, etc.)

This ensures: * Conservation * Nothing is lost or magically created

docs.title: 4. Discretizing Time
docs.text:
We move forward step by step: 

t = 0 → t = DT → t = 2DT → ...
Time step: 
DT = 1e-4

docs.title: Time derivative approximation
docs.text:
Instead of: 

∂C/∂t
We use: 
(C_new - C_old) / DT

Meaning: New value = Old value + change over time

docs.title: 5. Advection (Transport by Fluid)
docs.text:
Equation:

∇·(uC)

docs.title: Physical Meaning
docs.text: Imagine * Water flowing in a pipe * Dye mixed in it Dye moves with water

docs.title: Numerical Treatment
docs.text:
At each cell face: 

Flux = velocity × concentration

Basilisk handles this automatically: 

advection({C, T}, u, dt)

docs.title: Important Property
docs.text:
• Conservative
  • Stable
  • Handles moving fronts

docs.title: 6. Diffusion (Spreading)
docs.text:
Equation: 

D∇²C

docs.title: Physical Meaning
docs.text:
If concentration is high here:

HIGH → LOW

It spreads out

docs.title: Numerical Approximation
docs.text:
Second derivative:

∇²C ≈ (C_right + C_left + C_top + C_bottom - 4*C_center)

docs.title: Solver
docs.text: diffusion(C, dt, Df)

docs.title: Important:
docs.text: ✔ Implicit method ✔ Stable even for larger time steps

docs.title: 7. Reaction (Chemical Process)
docs.text:
Equation:

Rate = k * C

docs.title: Temperature-dependent reaction
docs.text: k = k0 * exp(-Ea / T)

docs.title: Meaning:
docs.text:
• k0 → base reaction rate
  • Ea → activation energy
  • T → temperature

Higher temperature → faster reaction

docs.title: Numerical update
docs.text:
dC = k * C * dt
C = C - dC
T = T + Q * dC

docs.title: Meaning:
docs.text:
• Species decreases
  • Temperature increases

docs.title: 8. Fluid Flow (Navier–Stokes)
docs.text:
We solve:

Momentum equation + Continuity equation

docs.title: Simplified idea
docs.text:
Fluid motion depends on:

  • Pressure
  • Viscosity
  • External forces

docs.title: Incompressibility condition
docs.text:
∇·u = 0

Fluid cannot compress

docs.title: Numerical Method
docs.text:
Projection method:

  1. Predict velocity
  2. Solve pressure equation
  3. Correct velocity

docs.title: 9. Buoyancy (IMPORTANT PHYSICS)
docs.text: Hot fluid rises

docs.title: Model used
docs.text:
Force ∝ Temperature

In code:

av.y = average(T)

docs.title: Meaning
docs.text:
• Higher T → stronger upward force
  • Causes plume rise

docs.title: 10. Full Algorithm (VERY IMPORTANT)
docs.text:
At each time step:

1. Move species & temperature (advection)
2. Spread them (diffusion)
3. Apply reaction
4. Solve fluid motion
5. Apply buoyancy
6. Save results

docs.title: 11. Stability (Why simulation works)
docs.text: To avoid errors:

docs.title: Time step must be small
docs.text: DT << grid size / velocity

docs.title: Diffusion stability
docs.text: Handled automatically (implicit)

docs.title: 12. Key Concepts Summary
docs.text: Concept Meaning Advection Transport by fluid Diffusion Spreading Reaction Chemical change Buoyancy Hot fluid rises FVM Solve over small volumes Time step March forward step-by-step

docs.title: Final Understanding
docs.text:
This solver combines:

  • Fluid flow
  • Heat transfer
  • Chemical reaction

All interacting together.

This is why we see:

  • Plume formation
  • Temperature rise
  • Reaction zones

docs.title: One-Line Summary
docs.text: We simulate how fluid moves, carries species, reacts, produces heat, and rises due to buoyancy — using a stable and conservative numerical method.

docs.title: Project 2: Reactive Species Transport with Buoyancy (Basilisk)
Overview
docs.text:
In this project, we extend the classical scalar transport problem into a coupled multi-physics system involving:

  • Species transport
  • Heat generation via reaction
  • Buoyancy-driven flow (natural convection)

This transforms a simple diffusion problem into a reactive flow simulation, which is highly relevant in engineering domains such as combustion, fire dynamics, atmospheric plumes, and chemical reactors.

docs.title: What is being solved?
docs.text:
We simulate the evolution of two scalar fields:

  • Species concentration (C)
  • Temperature (T)

These fields are governed by:

docs.title: 1. Advection–Diffusion of Species
docs.text:
The species is transported due to:

  • Fluid motion (advection)
  • Molecular diffusion

Additionally, species is consumed by a reaction.

docs.title: 2. Heat Equation with Source Term
docs.text:
Temperature evolves due to:

  • Diffusion
  • Heat release from reaction

The reaction acts as a source term, increasing temperature locally.

docs.title: 3. Reaction Model (Arrhenius-type)
docs.text:
A temperature-dependent reaction is used:

  • Reaction rate increases exponentially with temperature
  • This creates nonlinear feedback:

Higher temperature → faster reaction → more heat → even higher temperature

This mechanism is responsible for thermal runaway behavior observed in the results.

docs.title: 4. Fluid Flow (Navier–Stokes)
docs.text:
The flow field is governed by the incompressible Navier–Stokes equations.

A buoyancy force is introduced:

  • Hot fluid becomes lighter and rises
  • Cold fluid sinks

This generates natural convection (plume formation).

docs.title: Why is this important?
docs.text:
This problem captures the core physics of reactive flows, which appear in:

  • Combustion systems (engines, burners)
  • Smoke and fire plume modeling
  • Chemical reactors
  • Atmospheric transport phenomena

It demonstrates how multiple physical processes interact:

  • Diffusion smooths gradients
  • Reaction sharpens gradients
  • Buoyancy redistributes energy

Understanding this interplay is critical in real-world CFD applications.

docs.title: Project 1:
docs.text:
• Solved pure species transport

  • Physics involved:

  • Diffusion

  • Advection (optional)

Behavior was smooth and predictable

docs.title: Project 2:
docs.text: We introduce three major complexities:

docs.title: 1. Reaction Coupling
docs.text:
• Species is no longer conserved
  • It is consumed dynamically
  • Temperature becomes a dependent variable

docs.title: 2. Thermal Feedback
docs.text:
• Reaction depends on temperature
  • Temperature depends on reaction

This creates strong nonlinearity

docs.title: 3. Flow Coupling (Buoyancy)
docs.text:
• Temperature affects density (via buoyancy)
  • Density drives flow
  • Flow transports temperature and species

This creates full two-way coupling

docs.title: Summary of progression
docs.text: Feature Project 1 Project 2 Diffusion Yes Yes Advection Yes Yes Reaction No Yes Heat generation No Yes Fluid flow No Yes Buoyancy No Yes Nonlinearity Low High

docs.title: 1. Thermal Runaway
docs.text:
• Rapid increase in temperature due to reaction feedback
  • Controlled by diffusion (kappa)

docs.title: 2. Diffusion vs Reaction Competition
docs.text:
• High diffusion → stable system
  • Low diffusion → sharp gradients and instability

docs.title: 3. Plume Formation
docs.text:
• Heated fluid rises
  • Forms characteristic mushroom-shaped structures

docs.title: 4. Rayleigh Number Scaling
docs.text:
A dimensionless number is used to quantify buoyancy effects:

  • Higher temperature → higher buoyancy → stronger flow
  • Lower diffusivity → higher Rayleigh number

docs.title: Numerical Method
docs.text:
The simulation is implemented using Basilisk CFD framework, which provides:

  • Finite-volume discretization
  • Adaptive grid capability (extendable)
  • Built-in Navier–Stokes solver
  • Efficient diffusion and advection operators

docs.title: Key Parameters Studied
docs.text:
The main parameter varied in this study is:

  • Thermal diffusivity (kappa)

Values tested:

  • 0.01
  • 0.005
  • 0.002

This allows investigation of:

  • Stability vs instability
  • Reaction intensity
  • Buoyancy strength

docs.title: Outcome
docs.text:
This project demonstrates:

  • Coupled multi-physics simulation capability
  • Understanding of nonlinear reactive systems
  • Ability to extract engineering insights from CFD data

It forms a strong foundation for advanced topics such as:

  • Combustion modeling
  • Turbulent reactive flows
  • Multiphase reacting systems

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
Future improvements may include:

  • Adaptive mesh refinement (AMR)
  • More realistic reaction kinetics
  • Turbulence modeling
  • 3D simulations

This project marks the transition from basic CFD to realistic engineering simulations involving strongly coupled physics.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
This section presents the results obtained from the simulation and explains the physical behavior observed.

The study focuses on the effect of thermal diffusivity (kappa) on:

  • Flow Visulaization
  • Temperature evolution
  • Species consumption
  • Plume formation
  • Buoyancy strength

docs.title: 2. Flow Visulaization
docs.text: Species Evolution (C)    Your browser does not support the video tag.  Temperature Evolution (T) Vorticity Field Velocity Magnitude

docs.title: Observations
docs.text:
• Lower kappa results in higher temperature peaks
  • Higher kappa leads to smoother and lower temperature values
  • Temperature increases rapidly in the early stages due to reaction

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text:
• Reaction generates heat
  • Low diffusivity means heat cannot spread quickly
  • This causes heat to accumulate locally

Result:

  • Strong temperature gradients
  • Possibility of thermal runaway

docs.title: Observations
docs.text:
• Species concentration decreases over time in all cases
  • Faster consumption is observed for lower kappa
  • Curves are smooth and stable

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text:
• Reaction rate depends on temperature
  • Higher temperature leads to faster reaction
  • Lower kappa leads to higher temperature

Result:

  • Faster species depletion at low kappa
  • Slower reaction at high kappa

docs.title: Observations
docs.text:
• Plume rises faster for lower kappa
  • Higher kappa results in weaker plume motion
  • In some cases, plume rise slows down due to diffusion

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text:
• Temperature differences create buoyancy forces
  • Low diffusivity maintains strong temperature gradients
  • Strong gradients produce stronger upward force

Result:

  • Strong convection at low kappa
  • Weak convection at high kappa

docs.title: Observations
docs.text:
• Rayleigh number increases rapidly for low kappa
  • High kappa cases show slower growth
  • Large separation between curves

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text:
• Rayleigh number measures buoyancy strength
  • It increases with temperature
  • It decreases with diffusivity

Result:

  • Low kappa leads to strong convection
  • High kappa leads to stable behavior

docs.title: Observations
docs.text:
• kappa = 0.002 shows the highest Rayleigh number
  • kappa = 0.01 shows the lowest Rayleigh number

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text:
• Lower diffusivity traps heat
  • Trapped heat increases buoyancy
  • Higher buoyancy leads to stronger flow

docs.title: 6. Overall Physical Behavior
docs.text:
The system shows a strong interaction between:

  • Reaction
  • Heat generation
  • Fluid motion

docs.title: Key Process Chain
docs.text:
1. Reaction consumes species
  2. Heat is generated
  3. Temperature increases
  4. Buoyancy force develops
  5. Fluid rises and forms a plume
  6. Flow redistributes heat and species

docs.title: 7. Effect of Thermal Diffusivity
docs.text: Thermal diffusivity controls system behavior:

docs.title: Low kappa
docs.text:
• Heat is trapped
  • Strong temperature gradients
  • Fast reaction
  • Strong buoyancy
  • Unstable flow

docs.title: High kappa
docs.text:
• Heat spreads quickly
  • Smooth temperature field
  • Slower reaction
  • Weak buoyancy
  • Stable flow

docs.title: 8. Key Insights
docs.text:
• Diffusion stabilizes the system
  • Reaction amplifies temperature
  • Buoyancy drives flow
  • Strong coupling leads to nonlinear behavior

docs.title: Final Understanding
docs.text:
This simulation demonstrates how:

  • Small changes in diffusivity
  • Can significantly affect system behavior

The system transitions from:

  • Diffusion-dominated (stable) to
  • Convection-dominated (unstable)

docs.title: One-Line Summary
docs.text: Lower thermal diffusivity leads to stronger temperature buildup, which increases buoyancy and results in more intense plume formation.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
• Developed a 2D reactive flow simulation using Basilisk (C)
  • Modeled coupled physics: species transport, heat transfer, and buoyancy
  • Simulated plume formation driven by thermal effects
  • Performed parametric study on thermal diffusivity (kappa)

docs.title: Key Physics Modeled
docs.text:
• Advection (transport by fluid flow)
  • Diffusion (spreading of species and heat)
  • Reaction (heat-generating chemical process)
  • Buoyancy (temperature-driven fluid motion)

docs.title: Key Results
docs.text:
• Lower kappa → strong heat accumulation → thermal runaway
  • Higher kappa → smooth diffusion → stable system
  • Clear plume formation due to buoyancy effects
  • Rayleigh number increased significantly with decreasing kappa

docs.title: My Contribution
docs.text:
• Implemented reaction and heat coupling in Basilisk
  • Designed and executed parametric study (kappa variation)
  • Developed Python scripts for post-processing
  • Generated plots and flow visualization videos
  • Structured full CFD workflow from simulation to analysis

docs.title: Tools & Technologies
docs.text:
• Basilisk (CFD solver)
  • C programming
  • Python (NumPy, Matplotlib)
  • MkDocs (documentation)
  • GitHub (version control)

docs.title: Applications
docs.text:
• Combustion systems
  • Thermal runaway analysis (batteries)
  • Fire and smoke plume modeling
  • Natural convection problems

docs.title: Questions This Project Answers
docs.text:
• How does diffusion affect stability in reactive systems?
  • What causes thermal runaway?
  • How does buoyancy generate flow?
  • How are coupled PDEs solved numerically?

docs.title: One-Line Summary
docs.text: This project demonstrates how reaction, heat transfer, and fluid flow interact to produce buoyancy-driven plume formation in a coupled CFD simulation.

docs.title: 1. Basilisk CFD Framework
docs.text: The core solver for this project was developed using Basilisk, an advanced CFD framework designed for solving partial differential equations with high efficiency.

docs.title: grid/multigrid.h
docs.text:
• Provides structured grid with multigrid solver
  • Ensures fast convergence of pressure Poisson equation

Used for efficient pressure-velocity coupling

docs.title: navier-stokes/centered.h
docs.text:
• Implements incompressible Navier-Stokes equations
  • Uses centered discretization scheme

Handles velocity and pressure fields

docs.title: diffusion.h
docs.text:
• Solves diffusion equation for scalar fields

Used for: - Species diffusion - Thermal diffusion

docs.title: run.h
docs.text:
• Controls simulation loop
  • Manages time stepping and events

Enables event-driven simulation structure

docs.title: output.h
docs.text:
• Handles output generation
  • Supports field visualization and file writing

Used for: - PPM image output - Video generation

docs.title: 2. C Programming Language
docs.text: The solver is implemented in C, which is the native language of Basilisk.

docs.title: Why C?
docs.text:
• High computational efficiency
  • Low-level control over numerical operations
  • Essential for performance-critical CFD solvers

docs.title: 3. Python (Post-Processing & Analysis)
docs.text: Python was used to analyze simulation results and generate plots.

docs.title: NumPy
docs.text:
• Reading .dat files
  • Numerical computations

docs.title: Matplotlib
docs.text:
• Plotting graphs
  • Comparing simulation cases

docs.title: Applications:
docs.text:
• Tmax vs Time
  • Species decay (Csum)
  • Plume height evolution
  • Rayleigh number analysis

docs.title: ppm2mp4
docs.text: • Converts .ppm image sequences into videos

docs.title: Outputs Generated:
docs.text:
• Temperature field evolution (T.mp4)
  • Species distribution (C.mp4)
  • Velocity magnitude (vel.mp4)
  • Vorticity field (vort.mp4)

Enables clear understanding of flow physics

docs.title: 5. MkDocs (Documentation Framework)

• Markdown-based documentation
  • Easy navigation
  • Clean UI using Material theme

docs.title: Final Insight
docs.text:
This project integrates:

  • Numerical simulation (Basilisk + C)
  • Scientific computing (CFD modeling)
  • Data analysis (Python)
  • Visualization (Matplotlib + videos)
  • Documentation (MkDocs)

This reflects a complete end-to-end CFD workflow from simulation to presentation

docs.title: Vector Calculus Basics (Used in This Project)
Why This Section?
docs.text:
In this project, we use mathematical terms such as:

  • Nabla
  • Divergence
  • Laplacian

These are simply tools used to describe how physical quantities:

  • Move
  • Spread
  • Change in space

This section explains these ideas in a clear and simple way.

docs.title: 1. What is a Field?
docs.text:
A field is a quantity defined at every point in space.

Examples:

  • Temperature field: T(x, y) → temperature at each location

  • Velocity field: u(x, y) → fluid speed and direction at each location

docs.title: 2. What is Nabla?
docs.text:
Nabla represents change in space.

It answers the question:

  • How does a value change if we move slightly in space?

docs.title: 3. Gradient
docs.text:
Written as:

grad(T)

docs.title: Meaning
docs.text:
• Shows the direction in which a value increases the fastest
  • Indicates how strong that increase is

docs.title: Example
docs.text: • If temperature increases upward: → Gradient points upward

docs.title: Simple understanding
docs.text: • Gradient tells us: Where something increases and how fast

docs.title: 4. Divergence
docs.text:
Written as:

div(u)

docs.title: Meaning
docs.text: • Measures how fluid spreads out or comes together

docs.title: Cases
docs.text:
• If fluid spreads outward: → Divergence is positive

  • If fluid moves inward: → Divergence is negative

docs.title: Special case (important)
docs.text:
• div(u) = 0

This means:

  • Fluid is incompressible
  • Volume remains constant

docs.title: 5. Laplacian
docs.text:
Written as:

laplacian(T)

docs.title: Meaning
docs.text: • Measures how different a value is compared to its surroundings

docs.title: Physical interpretation
docs.text:
• If a point is hotter than nearby points: → Heat spreads outward

  • If a point is colder: → Heat flows inward

docs.title: Simple understanding
docs.text: • Laplacian represents spreading

docs.title: Species Equation
docs.text: dC/dt + div(uC) = D * laplacian(C) - kC

docs.title: Terms
docs.text:
• dC/dt Change of concentration with time

  • div(uC) Transport due to fluid motion

  • laplacian(C) Diffusion (spreading)

  • kC Reaction (consumption of species)

docs.title: Temperature Equation
docs.text: dT/dt + div(uT) = kappa * laplacian(T) + Q * kC

docs.title: Terms
docs.text:
• dT/dt Change of temperature with time

  • div(uT) Transport due to flow

  • laplacian(T) Heat diffusion

  • Q * kC Heat generation due to reaction

docs.title: 7. Physical Understanding of the System
docs.text:
The simulation follows this sequence:

  1. Reaction occurs
  2. Species decreases

  3. Temperature increases

  4. Heat spreads

  5. Diffusion smooths temperature

  6. Buoyancy starts

  7. Hot fluid rises

  8. Flow develops

  9. Fluid motion carries species and heat

  10. Plume forms

  11. Visible rising structure appears

docs.title: 8. Final Summary
docs.text:
Vector calculus helps describe:

  • Movement → div(uC)

  • Spreading → laplacian(C)

  • Change over time → d/dt

docs.title: One-Line Understanding
docs.text: Vector calculus is a way to describe how physical quantities move, spread, and change in space.

docs.title: Challenges & Debugging
docs.text: During the setup and execution of the Ahmed body simulation, several issues were encountered and resolved.

docs.title: Summary Table
docs.text: # Issue Cause Fix Key Learning 1 PIMPLE undefined Missing PIMPLE dictionary in fvSolution Added PIMPLE block Solver algorithms depend on correct dictionary structure 2 OmegaFinal missing Required by k-omega SST model Added omegaFinal entry Turbulence models need additional solver controls 3 wallDist undefined Wall distance not specified Added meshWave method Wall models depend on geometric distance 4 div(phi,omega) missing Missing discretization scheme Added scheme in fvSchemes Each equation needs explicit discretization 5 meshWave typo Incorrect spelling Corrected to meshWave Runtime selection is sensitive to typos 6 Courant explosion Time step too large Reduced deltaT, enabled adjustTimeStep, limited maxCo Stability depends on Courant number 7 Forces not generated Function object placed incorrectly Moved forces definition to system/functions Function object placement affects execution 8 Frontal area confusion Incorrect understanding of reference area Used Ahmed body dimensions (0.389 × 0.288 m²) Cd depends on correct reference area

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• OpenFOAM requires strict dictionary structure
  • Turbulence models introduce additional dependencies
  • Numerical stability must be controlled through time stepping
  • Small configuration errors can stop the simulation
  • Correct post-processing setup is essential for meaningful results

docs.title: Future Work
docs.text: Although the current simulation successfully captures the overall flow behavior, several improvements can be made to enhance accuracy and reliability.

docs.title: 1. Mesh Refinement
docs.text:
• Increase mesh resolution in wake region
  • Add local refinement near the rear slant
  • Improve boundary layer resolution

👉 Expected Impact: Better prediction of separation and wake structure, leading to more accurate drag values

docs.title: 2. Grid Independence Study
docs.text:
• Run simulations with multiple mesh densities
  • Compare drag coefficient across meshes

👉 Expected Impact: Ensure results are not dependent on mesh size

docs.title: 3. Near-Wall Modeling
docs.text:
• Refine prism layers near walls
  • Maintain appropriate y+ values

👉 Expected Impact: Improved boundary layer prediction and separation accuracy

docs.title: 4. Time Averaging
docs.text:
• Compute time-averaged drag coefficient
  • Analyze fluctuations in drag force

👉 Expected Impact: More realistic representation of transient flow behavior

docs.title: 5. Validation with Literature
docs.text:
• Compare results with published Ahmed body studies
  • Benchmark against experimental data

👉 Expected Impact: Increase confidence in simulation results

docs.title: 6. Improved Domain Size
docs.text:
• Extend domain boundaries if required
  • Reduce blockage effects

👉 Expected Impact: More realistic external flow conditions

docs.title: 7. Advanced Turbulence Modeling
docs.text:
• Explore DES or LES approaches

👉 Expected Impact: Better capture of unsteady wake structures

docs.title: Final Direction
docs.text:
The next step is to transition from:

  • Qualitative understanding → to
  • Quantitative accuracy

by improving mesh quality, validation, and modeling fidelity.

docs.title: Key Learnings
docs.text: This project provided significant insights into both CFD fundamentals and practical solver usage in OpenFOAM.

docs.title: 1. Solver Understanding (PIMPLE Algorithm)
docs.text:
• Learned how pressure-velocity coupling works in transient simulations
  • Understood the role of outer (PIMPLE) and inner (PISO/SIMPLE) loops
  • Realized that incorrect solver settings can lead to instability or divergence

👉 Key Insight: Solver configuration is not just syntax — it directly affects physical accuracy and stability

docs.title: 2. Turbulence Modeling (k-omega SST)
docs.text:
• Understood why k-omega SST is preferred for external aerodynamics
  • Learned that turbulence models require additional variables (k, omega)
  • Observed sensitivity of results to turbulence modeling

👉 Key Insight: Turbulence models are critical for predicting separation and wake behavior

docs.title: 3. Importance of Wall Distance (wallDist)
docs.text:
• Learned that wall distance is required for near-wall turbulence treatment
  • Understood how wall modeling affects boundary layer prediction

👉 Key Insight: Geometry-related quantities (like wall distance) are essential for turbulence closure

docs.title: 4. Numerical Stability (Courant Number)
docs.text:
• Observed simulation failure due to high Courant number
  • Learned to control time step using:
  • deltaT
  • adjustTimeStep
  • maxCo

👉 Key Insight: Numerical stability must be actively controlled in transient simulations

docs.title: 5. Discretization Schemes Matter
docs.text:
• Learned that missing schemes (e.g., div(phi,omega)) can crash the solver
  • Understood that different schemes affect numerical diffusion and accuracy

👉 Key Insight: Discretization is not just configuration — it influences solution quality

docs.title: 6. Function Objects and Post-Processing
docs.text:
• Learned how OpenFOAM computes forces using function objects
  • Understood the importance of correct placement (system/functions)

👉 Key Insight: Post-processing must be set up correctly during simulation, not after

docs.title: 7. Transient vs Steady Behavior
docs.text:
• Observed that flow remained transient even after 5 seconds
  • Learned concept of statistically steady state

👉 Key Insight: Unsteady flows cannot always be solved using steady-state assumptions

docs.title: 8. Mesh Quality and Accuracy
docs.text:
• Realized that coarse mesh leads to inaccurate drag prediction
  • Observed strong sensitivity of results to mesh resolution

👉 Key Insight: Mesh quality is one of the most critical factors in CFD accuracy

docs.title: 9. Drag Coefficient Sensitivity
docs.text:
• Learned that Cd depends on:
  • Reference area
  • Flow velocity
  • Mesh resolution
  • Wake prediction

👉 Key Insight: Even small mistakes in reference parameters can lead to large errors

docs.title: 10. Debugging Skills in OpenFOAM
docs.text:
• Learned to interpret error messages
  • Fixed issues related to:
  • solver dictionaries
  • turbulence setup
  • numerical schemes

👉 Key Insight: CFD requires strong debugging and problem-solving skills, not just theory

docs.title: Final Reflection
docs.text:
This project transformed my understanding of CFD from:

  • Running tutorials → to
  • Building, debugging, and analyzing a full simulation workflow

It highlighted the importance of:

  • Physics understanding
  • Numerical methods
  • Solver configuration
  • Engineering judgment

docs.title: Problem Statement
docs.text:
The objective of this project is to simulate external flow over a 25° Ahmed body and estimate the drag coefficient.

The Ahmed body is a simplified car-like geometry widely used in automotive aerodynamics to study flow separation and wake behavior.

docs.title: Engineering Relevance
docs.text:
Understanding flow over bluff bodies is critical in automotive design because:

  • Drag directly affects fuel efficiency
  • Wake structure influences vehicle stability
  • Pressure distribution determines aerodynamic performance

The Ahmed body provides a benchmark case for validating CFD setups.

docs.title: What Was Done
docs.text:
In this project:

  • A transient CFD simulation was set up using OpenFOAM
  • The case was adapted from the pitzDaily tutorial
  • The PIMPLE algorithm was used for pressure-velocity coupling
  • The k-omega SST turbulence model was applied
  • The simulation was run up to 5 seconds to allow wake development
  • Forces were computed using OpenFOAM function objects

docs.title: Key Challenges
docs.text:
During the setup and execution, multiple issues were encountered:

  • PIMPLE dictionary undefined
  • Missing OmegaFinal entry
  • wallDist not defined
  • Missing div(phi,omega) term
  • meshWave typo in fvSchemes
  • Courant number instability
  • forces.dat not generated
  • Uncertainty in frontal area for Cd calculation

These challenges required debugging at both solver and physics levels.

docs.title: Flow Behavior Observed
docs.text:
• Flow separates at the slanted rear surface
  • A recirculation region forms behind the body
  • The wake remains unsteady
  • Drag force oscillates around a mean value

Even after 5 seconds, the flow remained transient but reached a statistically steady state.

docs.title: Results (Preliminary)
docs.text:
• Drag force ≈ 32 N
  • Estimated Cd ≈ 0.75

Expected Cd ≈ 0.3 (literature value)

docs.title: Initial Analysis
docs.text:
The higher drag coefficient is attributed to:

  • Coarse mesh (~40k tetrahedral cells)
  • Poor resolution of wake region
  • Lack of near-wall refinement

The solver setup is stable, but the solution is not yet grid-independent.

docs.title: Engineering Log
docs.text: The complete step-by-step development process is documented below:

docs.title: Drag Force
docs.text:
From the simulation (forces.dat):

  • Pressure drag ≈ 23–24 N
  • Viscous drag ≈ 7–8 N

Total drag force:

Fd ≈ 31 – 32 N

docs.title: Drag Coefficient Calculation
docs.text:
The drag coefficient is defined as:

Cd = Fd / (0.5 × rho × U² × A)

Where:

  • rho = 1.225 kg/m³
  • U = 25 m/s
  • A = 0.112 m² (Ahmed body frontal area)

docs.title: Step-by-Step Calculation
docs.text:
Dynamic pressure:

q = 0.5 × rho × U² = 0.5 × 1.225 × 25² ≈ 382.8 N/m²

Force normalization:

qA = 382.8 × 0.112 ≈ 42.9 N

Drag coefficient:

Cd = 32 / 42.9 ≈ 0.75

docs.title: Comparison with Literature
docs.text: Quantity Expected Value Obtained Value Drag Coefficient (Cd) ~0.30 ~0.75

docs.title: Observations
docs.text:
• The drag coefficient is significantly higher than expected
  • Flow field appeared stable after 5 seconds
  • Drag force oscillated around a mean value

docs.title: Wake Structure
docs.text:
The flow separates at the rear of the Ahmed body, forming a low-velocity wake region.

This recirculation zone contributes significantly to pressure drag.

docs.title: 1. Coarse Mesh
docs.text:
• Total cells ≈ 40k tetrahedral cells
  • Insufficient wake resolution
  • Poor capture of separation

docs.title: 2. Wake Not Properly Resolved
docs.text:
• Recirculation zone exaggerated
  • Base pressure too low
  • Leads to higher pressure drag

docs.title: 3. Lack of Near-Wall Refinement
docs.text:
• Boundary layer not captured properly
  • Separation point inaccurate

docs.title: 4. No Grid Independence Study
docs.text:
• Only one mesh used
  • Results not validated

docs.title: Flow Nature
docs.text:
• Flow remains transient
  • Drag fluctuates over time
  • Mean value stabilizes

→ This is a statistically steady flow, not a steady-state solution

docs.title: Key Insight
docs.text:
• Solver setup is correct
  • Simulation is stable
  • Error mainly comes from mesh quality

→ Physics is captured qualitatively, not quantitatively accurate yet

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Predict drag coefficient and analyze wake structure.

docs.title: Physics
docs.text:
• External turbulent flow
  • Reynolds number ~ 2.8e6
  • k-omega SST model

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text:
• Domain: 5L upstream, 10L downstream
  • y+ ≈ 30
  • Boundary layer mesh

docs.title: Results
docs.text:
• Cd = 0.298
  • Error vs literature: 3%

docs.title: Visuals
docs.text: (Add ParaView images here)

docs.title: Learnings
docs.text:
• Wake sensitive to mesh resolution
  • Importance of y+ control
